text: The surgeon divides the splenic artery before approaching the spleen. The lesser sac is opened through the gastro-splenic ligament, and the short gastric vessels are clipped and divided.

The surgeon isolates the splenic pedicle and divides the splenic artery using absolute locking PDS clips. Dissection proceeds along the lower pole, where individual vessels are clipped and divided. The lower edge of the spleen is lifted with a flexible curved retractor to facilitate access to the hilum.
The surgeon repeats the clipping procedure at the splenic end of the vessel before dividing it, ensuring secure vessel management prior to further dissection.

The surgeon uses curved forceps with a flexible shaft to dissect a larger vessel with diathermy. Petal and forceps are maneuvered around the back of the vessel to ensure it is clear. A catgut ligature is tied externally around one end of the vessel, narrowing its diameter. The vessel is then doubly clipped with large metal clips and divided.

The surgeon proceeds with dissection towards the hilum of the spleen, elevating the lower pole using a retractor. Vessels and peritoneal attachments are stretched and safely dissected or divided, allowing adjacent structures to fall away from the spleen. The gastrosplenic ligament is opened, and curved forceps pass under the remaining pedicle. A linear thread lifts the pedicle, and a curved retractor draws the tail of the pancreas medially. The pedicle is assessed with a sizer before being divided with an endoscopic GIA stapler.

The surgeon continues dissection close to the spleen, clipping and dividing branches of the splenic vessels individually. The spleen is lifted away from its attachments using a retractor, revealing the lino-renal ligament. A second retractor applies counter traction, sweeping intraperitoneal structures medially, facilitating diathermy dissection under tension.

Under magnification, the surgeon clips small branches of the splenic artery individually while dissecting around the hilum. The tail of the pancreas is visible near the spleen, and care is taken to avoid damaging it during the procedure.

The surgeon lifts the spleen to divide the posterior leaf of the linoleum ligament, then places the spleen into a tough plastic intravenous infusion bag. The bag is inserted into the abdomen through a twenty-five millimeter cannula, with its edges brought up to the surface.

The surgeon breaks up the spleen digitally within the intravenous infusion bag and removes it in several pieces using sponge holding forceps. The suspended pedicle technique is utilized during the laparoscopic splenectomy.
